blocks.title: Training Package Prioritisation Strategy
blocks.description: The Training Package Prioritisation Strategy establishes a defined and transparent decision-making approach for initiating, prioritising, and managing updates to Training Packages. It outlines a mechanism to review Training Package products within an estimated five-year timeframe, maintaining their currency and relevance while responding to evolving industry needs in a timely and informed manner.

members.title: Director of Training Products and Quality
members.description:
Kirsty Waugh is an accomplished education specialist with more than 15 years’ experience leading the design of diverse learning programs within schools and VET. Her experience spans the private, not-for-profit and public safety sectors.

Prior to joining Public Skills Australia, Kirsty worked in the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector as the Manager Quality and Evaluation at the CFA. In this role, she supervised the design of learning courses and management of the Registered Training Organisation (RTO).

Kirsty’s unique blend of project management, stakeholder engagement and learning design set her apart as a dynamic leader in the field of education. She is a driving force behind the innovative solutions that empower learners across Public Skills Australia’s industry-sectors.

members.title: Executive Advisor
members.description:
Chloe Boyd is an experienced and passionate research and policy professional. In her 10 years’ experience, Chloe has worked across Policing, Correctional Services, Government and the Intelligence industry-sectors on diverse subject matters, including workforce planning, workplace transformation, sustainable service delivery, futures and foresight, family and sexual violence, and cybercrime.

In her tenure at the Australia New Zealand Police Advisory Agency (ANZPAA), Chloe’s work facilitating cross-jurisdictional networks honed her skills in information sharing, strategic advice and identifying solutions to common industry challenges. She is deeply committed to building effective and future-forward workforces in support of the critical work undertaken by the Public Safety and Government industry.

Chloe is currently completing her PhD in Criminology. Outside of work, Chloe is a strong advocate for supporting the community through volunteering. She is a former president of the Barwon Rotaract (Rotary) Club, and currently volunteers as a foster carer and events coordinator with Forever Friends Animal Rescue.

members.title: Chief Executive Officer
members.description:
Jean is a passionate leader with vast experience in the Policing, Fire and Emergency Services, and the Vocational Education and Training (VET) sectors. In her capacity as Chief Executive Officer at Public Skills Australia, Jean is perfectly positioned to drive positive change for our industry-sectors.

Jean’s career has spanned several sectors within Australia and South Africa, where she has led key projects in workforce planning, VET and higher education.

Previously, Jean worked as Director of Strategic Services at the Australia New Zealand Policing Advisory Agency (ANZPAA). During her 12-year tenure, she was responsible for the Innovation and Research team that focused on current and future forecasting, scenario planning and horizon scanning. She also led the development of the Police Training Package, and other Police training approaches for Australia and New Zealand

Prior to joining Public Skills Australia, Jean worked in the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector as Deputy Chief Officer at the Country Fire Authority where she led the operational doctrine and training portfolio.

While in South Africa, Jean worked with the Policing, Defence and Legal sectors at the South African Qualifications Authority (SAQA).

members.title: Director of Workforce Strategy and Insights
members.description:
Adam Halliwell is a highly accomplished professional with more than 10 years’ experience in the Victorian Public Service. Throughout his career, Adam has shown a commitment to undertaking evidence-based research and communicating complex data to a variety of audiences.

Prior to joining Public Skills Australia, Adam served as Acting Assistant Director of both the Innovation and Research and Policy and Projects teams for ANZPAA – one of our key member organisations.

Adam also enhanced his skills in data analytics, data capability and community engagement while working for the Crime Statistics Agency. During this time, he represented the agency on a community safety project that involved developing close partnerships with Victoria Police, the Department of Justice and other crime prevention agencies to support community engagement across Victoria.

Adam will oversee the workforce planning function for Public Skills Australia. He is motivated to enhance Public Safety and Government workforces in Australia and beyond.

members.title: Company Secretary, Governance Lead
members.description:
Jacqui Smith is a dedicated and seasoned Executive Support Officer with over three decades of experience across the Australian public safety and government industry. She brings experience from a wide array of organisations and peak industry bodies, including Victoria Police, Community & Public Sector Union and ANZPAA.

Jacqui's eye for detail and strong grasp of policy development is foundational in ensuring Public Skills Australia's work is compliant with our governance structures. Additionally, her strong leadership and alignment to our core values allows her to spearhead the development of many of our internal policies and initiatives.

As Jacqui continues to bring her prior knowledge and experiences across to Public Skills Australia, her firm grasp on the development of organisational policies and strong communication skills makes her a trusted member of the team.

members.title: Manager of Training Packages and Quality
members.description:
James Stroud is the Manager of Training Packages and Quality at Public Skills Australia, where he leads projects to update and enhance training packages across diverse industry sectors.

James brings extensive experience in education and training, having worked as a teacher, trainer, learning designer, and training package developer. His career spans a variety of contexts and industries, providing him with a deep understanding of pedagogy and its application within the Vocational Education and Training (VET) sector.

James is passionate about creating an accessible and inclusive VET system, empowering candidates from all backgrounds to engage and succeed in the sector. He is dedicated to ensuring qualifications align with industry capability requirements, equipping learners with skills and knowledge that are valued and recognised across industries. 

His expertise and commitment make him a vital contributor to Public Skills Australia’s mission of developing high-quality training packages.

members.title: Manager of Stakeholder Insights
members.description:
Paul Watson is a highly experienced strategic advisor who joins the team as the Manager of Stakeholder Insights. He brings decades of experience from across policy development, stakeholder management and enterprise planning.

Paul has previously been integral in the development of frameworks and plans for both private and public organisations. Passionate about ensuring positive stakeholder relationships, he has played a significant role in establishing strategic relationships with key stakeholders across a wide range of companies. His focus on accessible training and inclusive resources also ensures that all perspectives are captures throughout multiple projects.

Leading our Stakeholder Insights stream, Paul's previous experience and insights will play a major role in Public Skills Australia's growth.

projects.title: Firefighting Appliance Pump Technician
projects.shortDescription: This project will see the development of training products to align with the workplace outcomes of Firefighting Appliance Pump Technicians.

stages.stageDescription:
This project will see the development of training products to align with the workplace outcomes of Firefighting Appliance Pump Technician.

Firefighting Appliances are operational vehicles used by the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector. These appliances are designed to extinguish fires, facilitate rescues and provide various other forms of assistance. Consequently, the pumps utilised within Firefighting Appliances require specialised skills and knowledge for maintenance, troubleshooting and fault diagnosis.

In collaboration with the Australasian Fire and Emergency Services Authorities Council (AFAC), Public Skills Australia will identify the national practice standards of pump technicians and assist to build industry capacity of this workforce.

projects.title: Emergency Tree Operations
projects.shortDescription: This project will see the development of a Findings Report which will highlight the wide range of tree operations conducted by State Emergency Services (SES) and Fire and Emergency members, analyse their capability requirements, and consider the contextualisation of existing training products to meet these needs.

stages.stageDescription:
This project will see the development of a Findings Report which will highlight the wide range of tree operations conducted by State Emergency Services (SES) and Fire and Emergency members, analyse their capability requirements, and consider the contextualisation of existing training products to meet these needs.


Emergency Tree Operations require a wide range of skills, including an awareness of overhead hazards and the mitigation of risks within dynamic work environments. Due to the increase in severe weather events across Australia, these skills are in greater demand across the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector.


For the purposes of this report, the project will consider tree operations that include vegetation management practices in emergency contexts such as trimming, cross cutting and falling. This project will analyse existing training products that are available across different training packages for possible contextualisation within the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector.

During this phase, Public Skills Australia will collaborate with key stakeholders within the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector and other Jobs and Skills Councils to inform the development of the Emergency Tree Operations Findings Report.
This consultation phase will see Public Skills Australia engage with the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector to validate it's key findings for the report.
This phase will see the finalisation of the Emergency Tree Operations Findings Report with recommendations for future activities.

projects.title: Correctional Services Implementation Findings Report
projects.shortDescription: This project will help identify the targeted support Public Skills Australia can provide Registered Training Organisations (RTOs) in delivering CSC30122 Certificate III in Correctional Practice.

stages.stageDescription:
CSC30122 Certificate III in Correctional Practice is recognised by industry as the foundational qualification to be employed as an Adult Custodial or Community Corrections Officer. This qualification is provided through a range of contexts, usually divided into phases that consist of classroom-based learning, self-paced online modules, and on-the-job training.

This project will help identify the targeted support Public Skills Australia can provide Registered Training Organisations (RTOs) in delivering this important qualification. It will also examine supports that can be offered to candidates undergoing the training and assessment as well as provide recommendations to improve and promote the delivery and implementation of the qualification.

The scope of the project includes:

- compiling a Job Role Analysis to capture candidate and industry requirements for Adult Custodial and Community Correction Officers.
- identifying the challenges and barriers faced by industry, RTOs and candidates completing CSC30122 Certificate III in Correctional Practice.
- providing recommendations that strengthen the implementation and delivery of the qualification.

To inform the project, Public Skills Australia will directly engage with RTOs and academies to collect and analyse evidence-based insights into the implementation and delivery challenges that Corrective Services academies, RTOs and candidates are currently experiencing.

Please check the project page periodically for further updates.
For any queries about this project, please email the Training Product Development team at: tpd@publicskillsaustralia.org.au.
Details on consultation methodology and outcomes will be uploaded here when available.
Stakeholder feedback for this project will be uploaded here once available.
Further details on the finalisation of this project will be shared here when available.

projects.title: Local Government Skills Audit
projects.shortDescription: The Local Government Skills Audit is a national initiative led by Public Skills Australia to identify vocational skills gaps across Australian Local Government Areas.

stages.stageDescription: This project aims to build an evidence base to address workforce capability challenges and recommend improvements to training pathways for Local Government. The project includes a national audit of skills data, stakeholder consultations, and analysis of the uptake and utility of existing LGA VET training packages. It will map skills shortages against current training offerings and propose strategies such as curriculum reform, RTO engagement, and policy enhancements to strengthen the skilled labour pipeline and ensure future workforce readiness in the Local Government sector.

**Consultation and data collection**
Public Skills Australia is dedicated to ensuring stakeholder needs guide project processes, outcomes and recommendations. As such, Public Skills Australia will work collaboratively with State and Federal peak bodies, RTOs and all relevant stakeholders to build a quantitative and qualitative data set to articulate the skills needs and shortages with Local Government. We are committed to adopting a flexible and agile approach to data collection and consultation that represents the unique needs of Local Governments and their stakeholders.

**Data analysis and skills mapping**
The collection of quantitative data articulating the nature and volume of skills shortages will facilitate the mapping of these shortages against the content of LGA VET products. This will allow Public Skills Australia to determine training gaps within VET products and assess opportunities for increased agility in training delivery. The qualitative data collected will inform the project of the real-world challenges faced by Local Governments including barriers in accessing training, multi-role staff models and challenges faced by rural and remote Local Governments.
The final report detailing the project findings and recommendations will be submitted to the Department of Employment and Workplace Relations in June 2026.

projects.title: DEFEXO Defence Explosive Ordnance
projects.shortDescription: This project will see the development of a new unit of competency within the DEF Defence Training Package to support the Defence sector’s capability in testing and evaluating explosive ordnance.

stages.stageDescription:
This project will see the development of a new unit of competency within the DEF Defence Training Package to support the Defence sector’s capability in testing and evaluating explosive ordnance. This project has been identified in collaboration with the Department of Defence to support the safe testing of explosive ordnance and safeguard the wellbeing of its personnel within this capability. 

Initial engagements with subject-matter experts and stakeholders in the Defence sector have identified the technical components and requirements of this specialised unit of competency.

Public Skills Australia acknowledges the use of explosive ordnance units within the mining industry-sector and will engage with the Mining and Automotive Skills Alliance (AUSMASA), to identify relevant organisations for consultation. Public Skills Australia will work closely with the Australian Defence College to support the tri-service accreditation of Australian Defence Force staff and ensure the new unit of competency is effectively implemented.

#### Technical Committee

Engagement with the Defence sector is currently taking place. In accordance with the Training Package Products Development and Endorsement Process Policy, a Technical Committee has been stood up to provide advice on amendments to the DEF Defence Explosive Ordnance qualifications. The Technical Committee will provide expert advice, grounded in evidence to assist in the design and development of the training products.

#### Terms of Reference

The Terms of Reference for the Technical Committee can be found below:

[Defence Explosive Ordnance – TOR](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/1c8c4556-af3b-401b-85dc-1a63a5f68aa1)

Note: multiple members of the Technical Committee have had their identifying information omitted from the Terms of Reference.

Consultation Strategy
Public Skills Australia will engage in thorough consultation with stakeholders to ensure that their perspectives, concerns and insights are fully considered in the development process. Details of consultation, including mechanisms and identification of stakeholders can be found here:

[Defence Explosive Ordnance – Consultation Strategy](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/916a050c-e5db-44ed-9b1f-1197fdf5aa88)

#### 13 November 2024
The consultation period for the Defence Explosive Ordnance Project has now ended. 

Based on the feedback received, Public Skills Australia will collaborate with the project’s Technical Committee to determine if any revisions to the products are required.

The project's Consultation Log, which includes feedback and responses to feedback, is now available for download on this page.


#### 15 October 2024
Public Skills Australia has developed a draft unit of competency based on advice from the Defence Explosive Ordnance Technical Committee. 

This proposed new unit of competency, **‘DEFEXOXXX Conduct explosive ordnance test and evaluation processes'** has been designed to support the Australian Defence Force’s explosive ordnance testing and evaluation capabilities. Addressing the necessary skills and knowledge, it aims to assist in providing a safer environment for personnel undertaking ordnance test and evaluation activities. 

The unit covers identifying test requirements and methods, safety considerations, developing evaluation plans and drafting reports. 

This unit applies to Defence explosive ordnance test and evaluation supervisors, managers and team leaders but is relevant to any individuals who work in this field. It has been identified to be included as an elective unit within DEF41122 Certificate IV in Explosive Ordnance Proof and Experimental.

### We want your feedback

From 15 October to 12 November 2024, Public Skills Australia will conduct virtual workshops for public consultation with stakeholders on the proposed draft training products. PSA invites you to these consultations so you can share your feedback.

projects.title: Auslan, Interpreting and Translating Qualification Review (Phase 1)
projects.shortDescription: A review to assess the uptake, use and challenges relating to the Auslan, Interpreting and Translating qualifications.

stages.stageDescription:
## Phase 1 of this project is complete

The project team have finalised the [Auslan, Interpreting and Translating Qualification Review](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/a4b496b8-7882-413f-a5d5-7934baac2495.pdf) report. 

This recommendation report will inform Phase 2 of this project. You can view details of Phase 2 by [clicking here](https://publicskillsaustralia.org.au/projects/auslan-interpreting-and-translating-qualification-review-phase2).

The Auslan, Interpreting and Translating Qualification Review Project has been initiated in response to the Disability Royal Commission 2023 recommendation 6.2: 

*Increase the number of Auslan Interpreters and support the Deaf Census Report - Understanding Auslan.* 

Specifically, this project will explore:

- The utilisation of the Auslan training qualification, including factors that influence its adoption, accessibility, and relevance in the industry.
- The ability of Registered Training Organisations (RTOs) and Technical and Further Education (TAFE) to effectively deliver Auslan interpreter training, addressing challenges related to course content, delivery methods, and training capacity.
- The transition from training qualification to employment as an Auslan Interpreter, exploring the barriers to employment and identifying pathways to successful career entry in the interpreting field.

The project will be delivered in two phases:

**Phase 1**

This phase will be focussed on the development of a Findings and Recommendations Report to assess the uptake, use and challenges concerning the Auslan, Interpreting and Translating qualifications. It will identify key areas for improvement, and assess how the training can be better leveraged to increase overall use and effectiveness. 

Comprehensive insights and feedback, data will be collected through:
- Desktop Environmental Scan
- Facilitated workshops across Australia with key stakeholders

**Phase 2**

This phase will be aimed at the Review, Update, Promotion, and/or Implementation of the Auslan, Interpreting and Translating qualifications (hereafter referred to as Auslan qualification). It will focus on refining the relevant qualifications and promoting their broader use, delivery and implementation. 

The outcomes of this phase are expected to enhance the accessibility and quality of Auslan training, ensuring it meets the needs of both the interpreting community and the Deaf community.

This research stage explored the use of the Auslan qualifications, challenges for training providers and structure of delivery.

These workshops involved key stakeholders, including:
- peak industry bodies
- advocacy groups
- service providers

These workshops collaboratively developed content, and sought specialist input and guidance regarding relevant cultural considerations.

Insights from consultative workshops have been developed into a set of findings and recommendations.

projects.title: Police Crash Investigation and Analysis Review
projects.shortDescription: POL Police Training Package Release 10.0 will see the update of two qualifications within the Crash Investigation qualification stream.

stages.stageDescription:
### Project Overview

POL Police Training Package Release 10.0 will see the update of two qualifications within the Crash Investigation qualification stream. These updates will ensure that the products reflect the current and emerging requirements of police agencies. The Crash Investigation stream of qualifications outlines the skills of police officers to investigate and interpret road incidents involving a variety of vehicle types, pedestrians and other external environmental factors. This includes officers who manage incidents, gather evidence and provide their analysis for the investigation.

The update to the POL Police Training Package will be conducted in collaboration with the Australia New Zealand Policing Advisory Agency (ANZPAA), the peak body of the policing industry within Australia and New Zealand.

Through the update of the Crash Investigation qualification stream, Public Skills Australia will support the Policing industry by:

- Increasing the use of products and ensuring they are implementable.
- Improving police officers’ safety through alignment with current practices and processes.
- Enhancing the capability of Police personnel to use evaluation and analysis techniques for motor vehicle incidents.

#### Technical Committee

Engagement with the Police sector is currently taking place. In accordance with the Training Package Products Development and Endorsement Process Policy, a Technical Committee has been stood up to provide advice on amendments to the POL Police Crash Investigation and Analysis qualifications. The Technical Committee will provide expert advice, grounded in evidence to assist in the design and development of the training products.

#### Terms of Reference

The Terms of Reference for the Technical Committee can be found below:

[Technical Committee Terms of Reference](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/7778c379-9a2d-4ed2-b242-22f6a73c8a58)

Note: multiple members of the Technical Committee have had their identifying information omitted from the Terms of Reference.

#### Consultation Strategy 

Public Skills Australia will engage in thorough consultation with stakeholders to ensure that their perspectives, concerns and insights are fully considered in the development process. Details of consultation, including mechanisms and identification of stakeholders can be found here:

[Consultation Strategy](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/423c658c-96cd-4037-a4ee-06d40bf9080f)

#### Training Products in Scope

The review of the POL Police Training Package Release 10.0 include the following training products:

- **POL50120 Diploma of Crash Investigation**
- POLINV020 Collate and review crash investigation evidence
- POLINV022 Develop and present crash findings
- POLINV023 Examine a crash incident
- **POL60120 Advanced Diploma of Crash Analysis**
- POLINV016 Analyse complex factors involved in crash incidents
- POLINV017 Analyse crashes involving heavy vehicles
- POLINV018 Analyse crashes involving motorcycles
- POLINV019 Analyse crashes involving pedestrians and bicycles
- POLINV021 Critically analyse complex crash incident evidence
- POLINV024 Manage quality of crash analysis
- POLINV025 Present evidence on complex crash incidents

During the review, the packaging rules of the qualifications will be updated to ensure the units within the qualifications are all current and deliverable by Registered training Organisations.

#### Timeline

![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/2eaacf85-397b-4ae5-95a0-63063febe709)

For any queries about this project, please email the Training Product Development team at: tpd@publicskillsaustralia.org.au.

projects.title: POL Police Training Package Release 9.0
projects.shortDescription: POL Police Training Package Release 9.0 will see the update to several qualifications to meet the current and emerging requirements of the Police industry-sector.

stages.stageDescription:
POL Police Training Package Release 9.0 will see the update to several qualifications to meet the current and emerging requirements of the Police industry-sector. The update to the POL Police Training Package will be conducted in collaboration with the Australia New Zealand Policing Advisory Agency (ANZPAA). ANZPAA is the peak body for the Policing industry-sector within Australia and New Zealand and is representative of each policing jurisdiction. 

The qualifications identified for update in this project have been informed by the ANZPAA Annual Business Plan, approved by the Australia New Zealand Council of Police Professionalisation (ANZCoPP). The ANZCoPP Board comprises of commissioners from each state and territory and is inclusive of the Police Federation of Australia.  

This project will see the review of two vital operational streams within policing, investigation and intelligence.

#### Technical Committee

Engagement with the Police industry-sector is currently taking place. In accordance with the Training Package Products Development and Endorsement Process Policy, Technical Committees has been established to provide advice on the endorsement of training products.

blocks.title: Workforce Overview
blocks.content:
Across Australia, policing services are delivered by 8 jurisdictions.

Policing of the Australian Capital Territory (ACT) is conducted under an agreement with the Australian Federal Police (AFP), which directs ACT Policing under its portfolio.

columns:
The Police workforce is comprised of State/Territory Police organisations, as well as the Australian Federal Police. The industry sector employs fulltime equivalent (FTE) of sworn Police Officers of approximately 59,359 across Federal, State and Territories.

![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/269662b5-4cfc-4da9-99a0-dbaff3b42f35.png)

Police organisations and unions highlighted that they are experiencing shortages of Police Officers 
across all States/Territories. Additionally, stakeholders observed that national labour market datasets fail to capture the complexities of Police occupational shortages, overlooking factors such as government commitments, retention challenges, the impact of career progression, and the evolving demands of policing.

These shortages can be caused by regionality, competition for labour and recruitment and retention efforts, all of which are discussed in the Workforce Insights Report.

blocks.content:
Workforce Megatrends

In 2024, Public Skills Australia identified nine megatrends impacting across the Public Safety and Government industries. A brief overview of the key findings from 2024 are detailed below

blocks.title: Identified Challenges
columns: ![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/23bd916c-a164-47cd-85f9-72054fbaadb4.png)

#### Occupational shortages and operational capacity
Several drivers that may impact on Police occupational shortages and operational capacity that may not be fully understood due to analysis gaps.

#### Professional development and upskilling
The absence of comprehensive skills mapping impacts on the ability of public safety organisations to identify and address workforce skills gaps and occupational needs.

blocks.title: Strategy
blocks.content:
### Report Into the Drivers of Occupational Shortages and Impacts on Operational Capacity
Public Skills Australia proposes to develop a comprehensive analysis of the inputs and drivers of Police workforce shortages and operational capacity to inform the national evidence base. This will provide greater understanding of the factors influencing diminished operational capacity in the metropolitan and remote/regional/rural policing.

### (Proposed) Case Study Analysis of Skilling and Professional Development Profile Mapping 
Public Skills Australia proposes to deliver a case study analysis where a skilling and professional development profile has been overlaid with work force demographics to understand and identify Police skills gaps at a jurisdictional level. This work would seek to identify benefits and opportunities in adopting this approach to address gaps in skilling and professional development programs.

blocks.title: Policing Training Package
columns:
Policing is covered by the POL Police Training Package. Public Skills Australia collaborates closely with the Australian New Zealand Police Advisory Agency (ANZPAA) to review and keep this training package up to date.


All police staff members and personnel Australia receive general training through an Academy or College. They can then undertake additional training for specialist roles as they progress their career in policing.

Click [here](https://training.gov.au/Training/Details/?id=POL) to learn more about the POL Police Training Package.

faqList.question: Australian Federal Police Diversity and Inclusion Strategy 2023-2026
faqList.answer: This strategy showcases the AFP’s focus on promoting psychosocial safety in the policing workplace by ensuring that Police Officers feel safe, connected, respected, and engaged.

faqList.question: Australian Federal Police Strategic Workforce Plan 2022-2027
faqList.answer: The AFP’s Strategic Workforce Plan supports the agency’s commitment to developing and maintaining a skilled and capable workforce, driving innovation, and ensuring the secure delivery of AFP objectives.

faqList.question: Australian Federal Police (including ACT Policing) Corporate Plan 2024-2025
faqList.answer: The Australian Federal Police (AFP) Corporate Plan emphasises their commitment to protecting Australian livelihoods, and the Commonwealth by remaining agile, innovative, and accountable in an evolving and complex operating environment. The AFP aims to attract and develop a skilled workforce, advance capabilities through responsible exploration of new technologies like artificial intelligence and strengthen relationships with domestic and international partners to enhance effectiveness in addressing criminal threats.

faqList.question: Disability Inclusion Plan - 2023–2027 (TAS)
faqList.answer: This plan sets out the department’s goals and objectives for building upon and strengthening itself as a disability-confident workplace, and ensuring a supportive, positive and inclusive environment for all.

faqList.question: Keeping You Safe: Victoria Police Strategy 2023-2028
faqList.answer: The strategy gives an overview of the Victoria Police demographics, along with a 5-year vision that is predominantly focused on being visible in the community and building public trust and positive community perceptions of Police. Promoting a diverse Police workforce that is inclusive of First Nations and other cultures, as well as the LGBTIQ+ community is also a priority area, alongside addressing cybercrime and streamlining internal processes for effective and efficient outcomes.

faqList.question: LGBTIQA+ Action Plan - 2023-2026 (TAS)
faqList.answer: This Action Plan sets out expectations and goals for a three-year period to strengthen relationships with LGBTIQA+ communities and support employees, volunteers, and others who are a part of this community.

faqList.question: NSW Police Force. Our Focus Our Future: Connected Workforce Connected Communities
faqList.answer: This plan focuses on cultivating a strong community by ensuring the values and behaviours of the workforce are aligned to that of the organisation, in turn building public trust and perceptions.

faqList.question: NSW Police Force. ‘Be a cop in your Hometown’ initiative
faqList.answer: The ‘Be a Cop in Your Hometown’ initiative by NSW Police aims to strengthen regional policing by placing officers in their hometowns after graduation. This program enhances community engagement, workforce stability, and trust in law enforcement while promoting long-term sustainability and stronger local relationships.

faqList.question: NT Police. Our Plan 2024-2027 (DRAFT)
faqList.answer: This document outlines a strategic roadmap to enhance public safety and address evolving crime trends. The plan aims to create a more inclusive Police force reflective of the Northern Territory’s diverse demographics.

faqList.question: Queensland Police. Strategic Plan and Strategic Workforce Plan 2024-2028
faqList.answer: This Strategic Plan sets out an overview of the objectives for the Queensland Police Service for the next four years, focusing primarily on an effective workforce that efficiently delivers outcomes for the community.

faqList.question: South Australia Police. 2030 Strategy - Safer Communities
faqList.answer: This strategy emphasises the importance of having a robust and aligned workforce to ensure community needs are met, being guided by five principles.

faqList.question: WA Police Force. Agency Capability Review
faqList.answer: The WA Police Force Agency Capability Review (2023-24) calls for a clear long-term strategy to enhance operational effectiveness, workforce planning, and interagency collaboration.

faqList.question: WA Police Strategic Framework
faqList.answer: This strategic framework outlines the values, functions, priorities, and operational fundamentals that are integral to how WA Police conduct themselves whilst undertaking their core duties.

faqList.question: 2024 Core Skills Occupation List – Key Findings Report
faqList.answer: The Core Skills Occupation List (CSOL) provides labour market analysis that informs the identification of occupations that are considered appropriate for employer-sponsored temporary migration through the Core Skills Stream of the Skills in Demand (SID) Visa.

faqList.question: Australian Cyber Security Strategy
faqList.answer: The 2023 - 2030 Australian Cyber Security Strategy serves as a roadmap to achieve the Australian Government’s vision of becoming a world leader in cyber security by 2030.

faqList.question: Better Together - The Jobs and Skills Report 2024
faqList.answer: As part of JSA’s mandated obligations to provide insight on the Australian labour market and advice on workforce needs and priorities, this report details evidence aligned to five strategic pillars to enable a more prosperous economic future. Specifically, this report outlines JSA’s key current and emerging datasets that provide useful insight on the intricacies of Australia’s labour market. This includes, but is not limited to, insight on the current VET workforce, First Nations employment participation, regional workforce investment, emerging roles and employment projections.

faqList.question: Closing the Gap
faqList.answer: The Closing the Gap Report informs the development of recruitment, health and social programs specific to First Nations peoples. These programs are deployed across the Public Safety and Government industry-sectors.

faqList.question: Occupation Standard Classification for Australia (OSCA)
faqList.answer: Formally known as ANZSCO, the OSCA is a standardised framework for classifying occupations within Australia. As part of the replacement process from ANZSCO to OSCA in 2024, the ABS announced that OSCA will undergo regular updates in accordance with the OSCA Maintenance Strategy.

faqList.question: Occupational Shortage List (OSL)
faqList.answer: The OSL (formerly the Skills Priority List) offers an annual point-in-time review of the status of Australian occupations. Specifically, the OSL reports on whether occupations (at the four-digit and six-digit ANZSCO level) are experiencing shortages nationally and for each State/Territory in regional or metropolitan areas.

faqList.question: The Australian Government’s Strategic Priorities
faqList.answer: The Federal Government’s strategic priorities provide a four-year (2022 - 2026) policy direction for the Australian Public Service (APS) and sets targets across key areas. These key areas include the economy, public health and climate change.

faqList.question: The Clean Energy Generation: Workforce needs for a Net Zero Economy
faqList.answer: This series of reports considers emerging and future clean-energy workforce requirements as Australia strives to reach net-zero emissions by 2050. JSA’s preliminary modelling estimates Australia’s clean-energy workforce will need to grow from the current 53,000 workers to 84,000 workers by 2050.

blocks.title: Workforce Overview
blocks.content:
Correctional Services are an essential part of the criminal justice system. This workforce keeps the community safe and assists persons in custody to reintegrate into society. 

The Correctional Services landscape is multifaceted as each state or territory governs their own Correctional Services program. 

State and Territory Correctional Services include:
- adult custodial 
- community corrections 
- specialist services

Private prisons are managed by private industry.

columns:
Correctional Services is one of the three pillars of the criminal justice system that support and promote public safety in Australia. This industry-sector delivers custodial and community-based services aimed at rehabilitation and reducing reoffending to help keep communities safe.

The workforce spans a broad range of occupations, including frontline roles and supporting functions. This includes Correctional Officers (COs), who deliver custodial services in secure facilities, and Community Corrections Officers (CCOs), who supervise offenders serving non-custodial sentences or parole in the community. Community Corrections Officers typically engage in case management, risk assessment, and compliance monitoring. The broader workforce also includes public servants working inside and outside correctional environments across functions such as administration, education, policy, and intelligence
![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/334b8a1f-6efc-4f1f-a2bb-5f3684e9607e)

blocks.title: Identified Challenges
columns: ![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/9003c771-ca34-4449-ad6d-40068824a615)

#### Workforce Lifecycle
Research and consultation identified challenges contributing to increased workforce challenges impacting across all stages of the Correctional Services workforce lifecycle, including: 
- Attraction: attracting a workforce that reflects the needs of a contemporary corrections environment. 
- Recruitment: modifying entry requirements to meet recruitment targets, with a targeted focus on the recruitment of First Nations employees. 
- Onboarding: equipping employees for the complexities of the physical and psychological demands of a Correctional Services role through pre-service training.
- Retention: mitigating limitations on learning and development opportunities and effects of mental health impacts (resulting in increased psychological injury claims). 
- Exit / Separation: building a deeper understanding as to why employees exit, exploring exit/separation data limitations and assessing whether varying processes across organisations may provide additional insights.

#### First Nations Workforce 
High attrition and retention of First Nations employees is a challenge that is impacted by low participation rates of other employees in cultural awareness or competency training. Embedding cultural competency in organisations, with the objective of promoting cultural safety for First Nations employees, may see a positive impact on retention rates.

blocks.title: Strategy
blocks.content:
### Review of skills maintenance approaches throughout the workforce lifecycle
Explore existing skilling and professional development frameworks adopted by Correctional Services organisations, including non-accredited training, to identify opportunities for harmonisation of approaches.

### Proposal to investigate barriers to First Nations participation in the Correctional Services workforce
Draft a proposal to gain industry support to examine the factors that limit the participation of First Nations people in the Correctional Services workforce, including the reasons for joining, attritional factors and motivation for remaining in the workforce.

columns:
The CSC Correctional Services Training Package summarises the national standards for training within the Correctional Services industry-sector. 

The training package is delivered through enterprise and independent Registered Training Organisations (RTOs). Some Correctional Services jurisdictions also function as an RTO, allowing them to deliver the training as part of the CSC Correctional Services Training Package. This ensures that training for Corrections officers meets the agreed national standards. 

As a Jobs and Skills Council, Public Skills Australia plays an important role in maintaining this training package. Click [here](https://training.gov.au/Training/Details/CSC/summary) to learn more about the CSC Correctional Services Training Package.

faqList.question: Aboriginal Employment, Retention & Professional Development Strategy 2024–2029
faqList.answer: This strategy was finalised in 2024 and aims to recognise and support the unique contributions of Aboriginal staff within South Australia’s correctional system. It focuses on improving employment outcomes through culturally safe practices, professional development, and retention initiatives. The strategy is part of a broader commitment to reducing incarceration rates among Aboriginal people and fostering a culturally inclusive workforce.

faqList.question: General Skilled Migration Program Boost
faqList.answer: The Northern Territory’s General Skilled Migration (GSM) program supports the nomination of up to 1,600 overseas skilled workers annually across sectors including health, science, finance, agriculture, construction, IT, and hospitality. It offers both provisional and permanent visa pathways and aims to contribute $1.4 billion to the Territory’s economy by 2027. The initiative is part of the NT’s broader strategy to grow a $40 billion economy by 2030 and includes tools like WorkerConnect to match employers with skilled migrants.

faqList.question: Government of South Australia Department for Correctional Services, Aboriginal Strategic Framework 2020-2025
faqList.answer: The Aboriginal Strategic Framework aims to improve outcomes for Aboriginal people in the correctional system. It focuses on integrating Aboriginal cultural practices into services, enhancing employee cultural competence through training, and strengthening community partnerships for rehabilitation and reintegration. The framework aims to reduce Aboriginal overrepresentation in the justice system and improve cultural safety and support.

faqList.question: Government of South Australia, Department for Correctional Services, Safe at Work 2024-2026
faqList.answer: The 'Safe at Work' initiative is a key priority within the South Australian Department for Correctional Services (DCS) Strategic Plan 2022–2026, focusing on the safety of employees, partners, and individuals under DCS supervision. This initiative complements other departmental priorities, such as the '20by26' strategy, which aims to reduce reoffending by 20 per cent by 2026, and efforts to address the overrepresentation of Aboriginal people in the correctional system. The 'Safe at Work' initiative underscores DCS’s commitment to fostering a secure and supportive environment for all stakeholders.

faqList.question: Government of South Australia, Office of the Commissioner for Public Sector Employment, Anti-Racism Action Plan 2023-2028
faqList.answer: The South Australian Public Sector Anti-Racism Action Plan 2023–2028 outlines five key priorities: enabling employees to act against racism; embedding anti-racism into leadership and decision-making; strengthening inclusive and safe workplaces; ensuring communications reinforce the commitment to anti-racism; and maintaining accountability for anti-racism efforts. These priorities aim to proactively eliminate racial inequity and foster inclusion within the public sector.

faqList.question: NT Correctional Services Organisational Review – Executive Summary and Recommendations
faqList.answer: This review presents 481 recommendations to improve NT Correctional Services, including workforce development, leadership, and cultural safety. It identifies high attrition rates, unscheduled leave, and gaps in Aboriginal workforce representation, and proposes targeted reforms to address these issues.

faqList.question: Prison Workforce Strategy 2024–2034
faqList.answer: This 10-year strategy outlines a comprehensive plan to build a skilled, diverse, and culturally safe prison workforce. It includes initiatives for recruitment, retention, professional development, and leadership pathways, with a strong focus on Aboriginal workforce participation and wellbeing.

faqList.question: Queensland Corrective Services Mental Health Strategy 2022-2027
faqList.answer: The Queensland Corrective Services (QCS) Mental Health Strategy 2022–2027 aims to improve mental health support for individuals in custody by enhancing existing initiatives and establishing new ones. It emphasises case management and the development of health-focused facilities, like the Southern Queensland Correctional Precinct Stage 2, to elevate correctional mental health care standards. The strategy seeks to create a system that recognises and supports all individuals in custody.

faqList.question: Queensland Corrective Services, Innovate Reconciliation Action Plan July 2024 - July 2026
faqList.answer: The Queensland Corrective Services (QCS) Innovate Reconciliation Action Plan (RAP) aims to reduce the number of First Nations people in custody by fostering respect, equity, and equality. It focuses on building relationships with First Nations communities, ensuring culturally safe environments, increasing workforce representation, and maintaining governance and accountability through a RAP Working Group.

faqList.question: Recruitment and Retention of Aboriginal Staff – Report to Parliament
faqList.answer: This report investigates the barriers to recruiting and retaining Aboriginal staff in WA’s correctional system. It highlights high attrition rates, lack of cultural safety, and limited career progression, and recommends targeted incentives, mentorship, and inclusive workplace reforms.

Victoria Corrections Prisons and Parole, Safety Corporate Plan 2023─2027, Victoria Corrections Prisons and Parole, Victoria State Government, 2023.

Disclaimer

This data have been collected through consultation with industry-sector representatives as part of the Workforce Plan 2024: Correctional Services. These figures represent a snapshot in time and are subject to change. Public Skills Australia Limited has made every effort to ensure that the information and data contained in this report are accurate, up to date and have been sourced appropriately. Our organisation will update these figures annually as part of its Workforce Planning function. 

Correctional officers are also known as prison officers and custodial officers. Correctional officer has been used throughout this document as the preferred industry term and aligns to ANZSCO 442111. Community corrections officers are also known as probation officers, probation services officers, probation and parole officers, or parole officers. Community corrections officers has been used throughout this document as the preferred industry term and aligns to ANZSCO 411714.

Also note: The Australia and New Zealand Standard Classification of Occupations (ANZSCO) does not currently delineate between supervisory or managerial roles for the Correctional Services workforce, and State/Territory annual reporting data rarely distinguishes between supervisory and non-supervisory staff. As such, data on this cohort is not currently available.

Public Skills Australia has established several networks and subcommittees that our work will progress through. These groups provide key insights into the effectiveness of our training products.

How to join a subcommittee or network

Whichever way you choose to collaborate with Public Skills Australia, we invite you to join us to innovate and support our important industry-sectors.

If you have a particular interest in Workforce Planning or Training Product Development, there are several ways to get involved.

Contact us to nominate to join a subcommittee or network.

members.title: Board Director, Chair of the Audit, Finance and Risk Committee
members.description:
Tracey Linford has been serving as Public Skills Australia Board Director for the last three months. She comes to the Board with 41 years’ experience in policing in Victoria and Queensland. Throughout that time, she rose to the rank of Deputy Commissioner and served in General Duties, Crime, Intelligence, and Counter Terrorism portfolios. 

Tracey has extensive experience relating to Audit and Risk, Organised Crime, Domestic and Family Violence and Inclusion and Diversity through her work at the Executive level. She is currently working as a consultant providing advice on public safety in policing, justice and corrections. 
Tracey has received a Policing Excellence Award for Leadership from the Australasian Council of Women and Policing (ACWAP) and in 2014 was awarded an Australia Police Medal (APM) in the Australia Day Honours. She holds a Batchelor of Business & Commerce Degree and a Masters Degree in Public Administration.

members.title: Board Director, Chair of the Nominations Committee
members.description:
Scott Weber is a Public Skills Australia Board Director with nearly 28 years’ experience with the New South Wales Police Force.

Throughout his time with the New South Wales Police Force, Scott has risen to the rank of Sergeant. He worked at various Police stations throughout Sydney, and was active in regional, state, and national operations as a member of the Operations Support Group.

While still on active duty, Scott became involved with the Police Association of New South Wales, and was elected to the positions of Executive Member, Vice President, and then President. He was later appointed to the Board of the Police Bank during a time of significant culture and structural changes.

In 2013, Scott was elected to the position of Treasurer for the Police Federation of Australia (PFA), the national voice that represents more than 66,000 Police Officers across Australia. He then went on to become the Vice President in 2014, the Chief Executive Officer Designate in August 2018, and the Chief Executive Officer in October 2018, a position that he still holds. During his time at the PFA, Scott led numerous campaigns involving industrial, legal, political, and welfare issues involving members and the community.

Scott is also the Company Secretary of the National Police Memorial, a charity that works with the PFA to support the mental health and wellbeing of Australian Police Officers and their families.

members.title: Board Director
members.description:
Jo Crabtree is a senior executive and organisational leader with over 20 years’ experience driving people-centred transformation across complex and purpose-driven environments. Her career spans professional services, government, aviation, education and public safety, where she has led cultural renewal, organisational redesign, leadership capability programs, and major change initiatives.

As the Founder of ClearPath Workplace Solutions, Jo works alongside senior leaders and boards to build healthy, high-performing workplaces grounded in clarity, trust and shared purpose. Her approach blends strategic insight with deep human connection — activating leadership, strengthening culture, and creating sustainable systems that enable people and organisations to thrive.

Jo’s expertise is anchored in organisational culture and change, supported by a strong foundation in workplace relations, employee engagement, governance and organisational compliance. She has successfully led enterprise transformation, executive and talent strategies, enterprise bargaining, employee voice initiatives, and complex risk and people-governance programs.
Recognised for her values-based leadership and collaborative style, Jo is trusted for navigating high-stakes environments with empathy, commercial acumen and strategic clarity.

Her core areas of expertise include:
•	Organisational Culture & Transformation
•	Leadership Development & Psychological Safety
•	Strategic Workforce Planning & Talent Systems

Jo is currently completing professional accreditation through the Resolution Institute, reflecting her commitment to building capability in mediation, constructive conflict resolution, and modern people practice.

Based in Melbourne, Jo brings energy, warmth and a deep commitment to supporting organisations that enrich communities and help people realise their best.

blocks.title: Workforce Overview
blocks.content: Local Governments play a significant role in building and maintaining cohesive and connected communities. This industry-sector has a broad remit, such as local infrastructure, town planning and land development. The breadth of these core services demonstrates that Local Government is an industry-sector that employs occupations from across the labour market, requiring a range of skills and training to support this workforce.

columns:
There are 537 Local Governments across Australia which are primarily concentrated on the east coast. Within these Local Governments, the top employing occupation is administration, with 151,000 employed across Australia.
![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/c40e3c21-6474-4a7e-b378-022c93fbff4c)

Occupational shortages are also present within the Local Government industry-sector, in particular, engineers, town planners, building surveyors, environmental health officers, accountants and human resources professionals. 

![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/ca9b4b4e-333d-4611-b711-52ebda8d7be8)

These shortages can be caused by regionality, competition for labour and recruitment and retention efforts, all of which are discussed in the Workforce Insights Report.

blocks.content:
Workforce Megatrends

In 2024, Public Skills Australia identified nine megatrends impacting across the Public Safety and Government industries. A brief overview of the key findings from 2024 are detailed below

blocks.title: Identified Challenges
columns: ![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/0fad702e-d6d7-4f02-9b48-d602885d6489)

#### Access to Training
Local Government workforces in regional, rural and remote areas are often limited in where and how they can access training needs. This includes limited access to RTOs that offer the LGA Local Government Training Package, directly impacting the uptake of this Training Package

#### Skills Gaps
The Local Government industry-sector is facing significant occupational shortages in certain occupations that require long training pathways, such as urban and regional planners, prompting growing interest in training products that require shorter timeframes for delivery for faster upskilling of priority areas. One key occupational area experiencing significant skills gaps is emergency management.

#### Resourcing and Recruitment
Access to adequate and affordable housing and social infrastructure remain key challenges in recruiting Local Government workforces in regional, rural and remote areas. Financial constraints imposed by other tiers of government also impact on the ability of Local Governments to sustain resourcing.

blocks.title: Strategy
blocks.content:
### Skill Set Development for Priority Skilling Areas
Identify whether any existing training products can be used for priority skilling needs in Local Government. Should no training products exist, training products may be developed to support the priority skilling needs in Local Government.

blocks.title: Local Government Training Package
columns:
The LGA Local Government Training Package (the training package) provides training and development for employees working in Local Government roles. It includes a range of qualifications and units of competency that cover aspects of local government operations, such as governance, community engagement, environmental management, and regulatory services. The training package aims to ensure that local government employees have the necessary skills and knowledge to perform their roles effectively.

As a Jobs and Skills Council, Public Skills Australia plays an important role in maintaining this training package.

Click [here](https://training.gov.au/Training/Details/LGA/summary) to learn more about the LGA Local Government Training Package.

![](https://psa-directus-prod.azurewebsites.net/assets/a4573ebd-0b34-419f-ac41-567470229954)

faqList.question: 2023 Local Government Workforce Shortages Survey in Western Australia
faqList.answer: The 2023 Local Government Workforce Shortage Survey in Western Australia, conducted by Local Government Professionals WA and the McCusker Centre UWA, reveals significant recruitment and retention challenges. Key issues include skills shortages, geographic barriers, and competition from other industry-sectors. Effective strategies include using social media for recruitment and employee reskilling. The survey recommends building a resilient workforce through flexible and targeted training to support Local Governments in addressing workforce shortages and enhancing community service delivery.

faqList.question: 2022 Local Government Workforce Skills and Capability Survey: Final Report
faqList.answer: The 2022 Local Government and Workforce Skills and Capability Survey: Final Report provides insights into Local Government workforce demographics and qualification profiles. The survey also highlights workforce challenges, particularly skills and occupational shortages, and outlines strategies to address them.

faqList.question: National Housing Accord
faqList.answer: The Commonwealth, as well as all State/Territory governments agreed to build 1.2 million homes, over a five-year period, commencing in mid-2024. It is a component of the government’s housing agenda to support social housing and additional support for renters and homebuyers. The National Cabinet has endorsed the Commonwealth by providing $3.5 billion in payments to state, territory and local governments to support the delivery of this accord.

faqList.question: The Inquiry into Local Government Sustainability
faqList.answer:
This inquiry is currently being undertaken federally by a House of Representatives Committee. The Inquiry into Local Government Sustainability gathered feedback from Local Government stakeholders on issues such as: 

- Financial sustainability and funding 
- Evolving infrastructure and service delivery obligations 
- Structural barriers affecting Local Government workers and services 
- Trends in attracting and retaining skilled workers, including labour hire impacts.

faqList.question: 2024 Core Skills Occupation List – Key Findings Report
faqList.answer:
The Core Skills Occupation List (CSOL) provides labour market analysis 
that informs the identification of occupations that are considered 
appropriate for employer-sponsored temporary migration through 
the Core Skills Stream of the Skills in Demand (SID) Visa.

faqList.question: Australian Cyber Security Strategy
faqList.answer: The 2023 - 2030 Australian Cyber Security Strategy serves as a roadmap to achieve the Australian Government’s vision of becoming a world leader in cyber security by 2030.

faqList.question: Better Together - The Jobs and Skills Report 2024
faqList.answer: As part of JSA’s mandated obligations to provide insight on the Australian labour market and advice on workforce needs and priorities, this report details evidence aligned to five strategic pillars to enable a more prosperous economic future. Specifically, this report outlines JSA’s key current and emerging datasets that provide useful insight on the intricacies of Australia’s labour market. This includes, but is not limited to, insight on the current VET workforce, First Nations employment participation, regional workforce investment, emerging roles and employment projections.

faqList.question: Closing the Gap
faqList.answer: The Closing the Gap Report informs the development of recruitment, health and social programs specific to First Nations peoples. These programs are deployed across the Public Safety and Government industry-sectors.

faqList.question: Occupation Standard Classification for Australia (OSCA)
faqList.answer: Formally known as ANZSCO, the OSCA is a standardised framework for classifying occupations within Australia. As part of the replacement process from ANZSCO to OSCA in 2024, the ABS announced that OSCA will undergo regular updates in accordance with the OSCA Maintenance Strategy.

faqList.question: Occupational Shortage List (OSL)
faqList.answer: The OSL (formerly the Skills Priority List) offers an annual point-in-time review of the status of Australian occupations. Specifically, the OSL reports on whether occupations (at the four-digit and six-digit ANZSCO level) are experiencing shortages nationally and for each State/Territory in regional or metropolitan areas.

faqList.question: The Australian Government’s Strategic Priorities
faqList.answer: The Federal Government’s strategic priorities provide a four-year (2022 - 2026) policy direction for the Australian Public Service (APS) and sets targets across key areas. These key areas include the economy, public health and climate change.

faqList.question: The Clean Energy Generation: Workforce needs for a Net Zero Economy
faqList.answer: This series of reports considers emerging and future clean-energy workforce requirements as Australia strives to reach net-zero emissions by 2050. JSA’s preliminary modelling estimates Australia’s clean-energy workforce will need to grow from the current 53,000 workers to 84,000 workers by 2050.

blocks.title: Our Purpose
blocks.description: Who we are and what we do.

blocks.content:
What are Jobs and Skills Councils (JSCs)?

JSCs are not-for-profit organisations that work with their industry-sectors to identify current workforce challenges or potential skills shortages. They develop and review relevant training packages and support the implementation, monitoring and promotion of vocational education and training (VET) within their industry-sectors. 

Every JSC is industry-owned and brings together employer and employee bodies as well as governments in a tripartite arrangement to workforce and training challenges.

JSCs are responsible for the following four key functions:

columns: ### Workforce Planning Workforce Planning explores the challenges, risks, and current datasets that encompass the unique workforces of relevant industry sectors.

### Training Product Development Training Product Development is key to addressing identified workforce challenges. It allows for regular reviews and new developments within training packages to ensure that training remains contemporary and fit for purpose.

### Implementation, Promotion and Monitoring Implementation, Promotion and Monitoring supports the delivery and effectiveness of training packages, and supports learners and Registered Training Organisations.
### Industry Stewardship Industry Stewardship requires JSCs to collect relevant information that guides the development of important policies, guidelines, and strategies.

blocks.title: How do JSCs work?
blocks.content:
JSCs are funded through Grant Agreements with the Department of Employment and Workplace Relations (DEWR). They work collaboratively with their industry-sectors and other stakeholders such as Jobs and Skills Australia to share industry intelligence and further the aims of the VET reforms. 

All JSCs are governed through an elected Board, and hold Annual General Meetings with its ordinary and affiliate memberships.

blocks.title: What is Public Skills Australia?
blocks.content:
Public Skills Australia is the JSC that works with the Public Safety and Government industry-sectors.

We play a pivotal role in responding to the workforce challenges and skills needs of these industry-sectors by conducting comprehensive skills assessments, listening deeply with our networks, and accelerating the introduction of new skills into the job market. 

We aim to promote innovation in Public Safety and Government workforces, and identify potential shortages that our industry-sectors may face in the future.

blocks.title: Collaborating with our partners and members
blocks.content:
Public Skills Australia values thorough consultation with its key industry and departmental stakeholders. This allows us to understand the workforce obstacles and training needs that are specific to each industry-sector.

We engage with and gather feedback from our stakeholders across our projects and activities through: 
- participating in regular engagements
- conducting direct consultations
- delivering webinars
- creating surveys
- running and participating in workshops 
- attending industry-specific conferences and events. 

These approaches are implemented across all our subcommittees and networks that we work closely with to inform our work. Representatives from our industry-sectors have been invited to join our membership, or to participate in one of our subcommittees or networks. 

Click [here](https://psa-website-beta.azurewebsites.net/our-governance) to learn more about these invaluable groups.

faqList.question: How do I establish a working relationship with Public Skills Australia?
faqList.answer:
Organisations can establish a working relationship with Public Skills Australia by joining a relevant subcommittee or network. Visit Subcommittees and Networks to find out more about our governance groups.  

Get in touch with us via the contact form on our website or subscribe to our mailing list to find out more about our upcoming projects.

We also attend relevant conferences across the country. Follow us on LinkedIn to find out where you can see us next.

faqList.question: How do I subscribe to receive updates?
faqList.answer:
We regularly provide updates on our projects and published reports through our membership portal. By signing up to the Public Skills Australia membership platform, you can register your interest in our different projects and receive updates on our work. This platform will be launched in 2025.  

In the meantime, follow us on LinkedIn and stay up to date with all things Public Skills Australia.

faqList.question: Which training packages are Public Skills Australia responsible for?
faqList.answer:
Public Skills Australia is responsible for reviewing and maintaining the following training packages: 

- • CSC Correctional Services
- • DEF Defence 
- • LGA Local Government
- • POL Police
- • PSP Public Sector
- • PUA Public Safety

faqList.question: What is Workforce Planning?
faqList.answer:
One of Public Skills Australia’s core functions as a JSC, Workforce Planning aims to understand current and future workforce challenges and skills gaps to develop strategies in line with industry needs. 

Each year, the Workforce Planning team produces a unique workforce plan for each industry-sector. The key findings from the workforce plans inform the other core JSC functions, including Training Product Development and Industry Stewardship.

faqList.question: Why is Workforce Planning important?
faqList.answer:
Workforce Planning provides strategic direction for Public Skills Australia’s other core functions as a JSC. These are: 


- •	Training Product Development 
- •	Implementation, Promotion and Monitoring
- •	Industry Stewardship functions. 


The identified skills gaps and workforce challenges allow Public Skills Australia to develop more targeted training products and share knowledge with industry.

faqList.question: What methodology does Workforce Planning use?
faqList.answer: The Workforce Planning team used a mix of industry and federal datasets, and industry consultation to develop the workforce plans. Data from Jobs and Skills Australia, the Australian Bureau of Statistics and annual reports were used to create an accurate picture of the Public Safety and Government industry in 2024.

faqList.question: How have the Workforce Plans been implemented?
faqList.answer:
The key findings from the workforce plans have enabled Public Skills Australia to provide critical feedback to the Australian Bureau of Statistics on behalf of our industry to inform the Australia and New Zealand Standard Classification of Occupations (ANZSCO). 

Additionally, the identified skills gaps have been used to provide feedback on the national Skills Priority List that is published by Jobs and Skills Australia.

faqList.question: How can I get involved with the next workforce plans?
faqList.answer:
Public Skills Australia develops workforce plans annually in line with our Grant Agreement with DEWR. 
 
Consultation with industry is conducted year-round, so get in touch to provide your input for the next workforce plans.

blocks.content:
This project aims to review and enhance Defence Public Affairs qualifications to equip personnel with the skills necessary for effective engagement with the media, stakeholders and the public. Considering the critical role of public trust in the Defence sector, this initiative underscores the importance of transparency, reputation management and strategic communication.

Through benchmarking, collaboration with industry experts and establishment of a Technical Committee, the initiative will update Defence Public Affairs qualifications to ensure continued alignment with industry best practices and evolving requirements within Defence.

Technical Committee

Engagement with the Defence sector is currently taking place. In accordance with the Training Package Products Development and Endorsement Process Policy, a Technical Committee has been stood up to provide advice on amendments to the Defence Public Affairs Qualifications.

The Terms of Reference for the Technical Committee can be found below:

 

[Defence Public Affairs – Technical Committee TOR](Defence Public Affairs - Technical Committee ToR)

Consultation Strategy

Public Skills Australia will engage in thorough consultation with stakeholders to ensure that their perspectives, concerns and insights are thoroughly considered in the development process. Details of consultation, including mechanisms and identification of stakeholders, can be found below:

To view the consultation strategy for this project, please click below:


[
Defence Public Affairs – Consultation Strategy
](Defence Public Affairs Qualifications Review - Consultation Strategy)

Training Products in Scope

Throughout this project, a consultation log will be maintained to document the feedback received. Information on consensus gathering will be published following the consultation phase.

Details of this project’s information sessions and workshops will be shared on the Consultation tab when available.

This project will review three qualifications, their core units of competency, elective streams and packaging rules. Through this review, the broader learning pathways and qualification design will be considered, noting the key focus in these qualifications is to support Defence capability. 

The review will consider the potential for streamlining training products and enhancing understanding of career progression between these three qualifications.

These qualifications include:

• DEF30417 Certificate III in Defence Public Affairs

• DEF40117 Certificate IV in Defence Public Affairs

• DEF50517 Diploma of Defence Public Affairs.

A comprehensive review of the Defence Public Affairs qualification stream has been undertaken in collaboration with a project Technical Committee, representative of the three services of the Australian Defence Force and the Australian Defence College. 

The proposed updates to the qualifications have been designed to address several factors aimed to support the Defence sector and improve the operational value of the qualification stream.

These factors include:

  • Responding to the emerging Defence sector needs of the qualifications
  • Increasing the implementation of the qualifications
  • Designing the qualifications to recognise transferable skills and prior knowledge
  • Reviewing the career pathways of candidates entering and exiting the qualifications
  • Considering the Australian Qualification Framework requirements for each qualification level
  • Summary of proposed updates

Please see below for a high-level summary of proposed changes to be made to the Defence Public Affairs qualifications.

Certificate IV in Defence Public Affairs

The proposed changes to the Certificate IV in Defence Public Affairs include updates to the:

  • Total number of units of competency required for the qualification reduced from 14 to 11.
  • Packaging rules have been updated to increase the amount of core units of competency required.
  • Imported of units of competency updated
  • Qualification description refined

Diploma of Defence Public Affairs

The proposed changes to the Diploma of Defence Public Affairs include updates to the:

  • Total number of units required for the qualification increased from 11 to 13
  • Packaging rules have been updated to increase the amount of core units of competency required.
  • Imported of units of competency updated
  • Qualification description refined
  • Minor editorial updates to Defence Public Affairs Units of Competency

The following units of competency have had minor editorial changes to ensure consistency in grammar and language across their contents.

  • DEFDPA001 Promote the organisation’s mission and values
  • DEFDPA002 Identify material suitable for media publication
  • DEFDPA003 Conduct market profiling
  • DEFDPA004 Manage media requirements at major events
  • - DEFDPA005 Develop and implement media plans
  • DEFDPA006 Develop and implement a public affairs strategic plan
  • DEFDPA007 Develop and implement effective communication strategies
  • DEFDPA008 Manage public affairs projects
  • DEFDPA009 Apply knowledge of public affairs in a service environment

We want your feedback

Consultation on the proposed changes will be open from 1 October to 29 October 2024. During this timeframe, Public Skills Australia will conduct virtual workshops for public consultation with stakeholders on the proposed draft training products.

Public Skills Australia invites you to these consultations so you can share your feedback.

Workshops Schedule:

  • Session 1: 23 October 2024, 11:00am – 12:00pm AEDT
  • Session 2: 30 October 2024, 11:00am – 12:00pm AEDT

Documents
Please use the form below to specify the qualification(s) or unit(s) of competency you wish to review and to register for the workshops.

Click [here](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=0kV-uiZxa0Kt3xs9pY_Vpj4frnF_SPtMtlsjZwSpVQRUMldOTDQyNVRNUU1NTUxRSElUS0dYM0tSWiQlQCN0PWcu&route=shorturl) to register for any of our workshops and receive the Draft Training Products pack.

Upon reviewing the Draft Training Products pack, please click [here] to submit your feedback.

If you prefer to provide your feedback via email, or have any further questions, please contact our Training Product Development team at:

tpd@publicskillsaustralia.org.au.

Project Overview

This project will see the development of a Findings Report which will highlight the wide range of tree operations conducted by State Emergency Services (SES) and Fire and Emergency members, analyse their capability requirements, and consider the contextualisation of existing training products to meet these needs.


Emergency Tree Operations require a wide range of skills, including an awareness of overhead hazards and the mitigation of risks within dynamic work environments. Due to the increase in severe weather events across Australia, these skills are in greater demand across the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector.


For the purposes of this report, the project will consider tree operations that include vegetation management practices in emergency contexts such as trimming, cross cutting and falling. This project will analyse existing training products that are available across different training packages for possible contextualisation within the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector.

Consultation

During this phase, Public Skills Australia will collaborate with key stakeholders within the Fire and Emergency Services industry-sector and other Jobs and Skills Councils to inform the development of the Emergency Tree Operations Findings Report.
